# Practical Language Techniques

**Technique 1: Replace "I think" with "I intend to." This shifts from passive to active.**

**Technique 2: Use "I learned" instead of "I was wrong." This normalizes learning.**

**Technique 3: Ask "What do you intend?" instead of "What do you think?"**

**Case: The Ship Captain. Changing how the captain asked questions changed how the crew responded.**

**Case: The Team Meeting. Structured bluework sessions created better decisions.**

**Case: The After-Action Review. Complete the cycle with structured reflection.**

**Case: The Brief. A structured brief uses "I intend to" to clarify roles and responsibilities.**
**Case: The Decision Space. Clearly communicate the boundaries of decision-making authority.**
**Case: The Debrief. A structured debrief completes the cycle. What did we learn?**
**Case: The Check-In. A quick connection check-in before starting work builds trust.**
**Case: The Repeat Back. Have team members repeat instructions in their own words to confirm understanding.**
**Case: The Open Question. Ask open questions that require thinking, not yes/no answers.**
**Case: The Visualization. Before action, visualize the process. This connects bluework to redwork.**
**Case: The Pause. A deliberate pause between redwork and bluework resets the mode.**
